facade_colour: bright yellow-painted brick facade with green-red trim accents

hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property has value as an early-twentieth-century vernacular style two-storey commercial addition to a nineteenth-century residential structure. Through its construction date and typology, the property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed between 1924 and 1930, the property has retained its brick cladding, second storey window openings with cast stone sills and brick surrounds, cornice, and cast stone capped pediment with stone coat of arms. Contextually, the property supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

context.street_character: One-way WB | 30 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks & roadway | E-W commercial connector

assessment.signage: HOUSE OF MOSES (arched lettering on storefront fascia)

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: bright yellow-painted brick facade with green-red trim accents
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Facade is vividly repainted and actively occupied; lower storefront and trim show ad hoc repairs and repainting.
photo_observations.window_details: Two upper domestic-style windows above a storefront composed of one entry door and one broad display window.
photo_observations.door_details: One white-painted side door and one glazed retail entry under the arched sign.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Modest projecting eaves and painted trim at the top of the facade.
photo_observations.porch_details: Low painted step-up and narrow recessed entrance zone at the storefront.
photo_observations.chimney_details: No chimney clearly visible from the frontal view.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Bright arched sign panel and layered paint emphasize the house-form facade.
photo_observations.roof_type_observed: Flat-topped or very shallow roofline behind a painted front parapet
photo_observations.storefront_description: Retail storefront inserted into a brightly painted two-storey brick building.
photo_observations.signage_observed: House of Moses arched sign with establishment date.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Strong repainting and later storefront insertion alter the original residential/commercial expression.
photo_observations.notes: The neighboring modern building at 160 Baldwin is visible immediately beside it.

storefront_description: Retail storefront inserted into a brightly painted two-storey brick building.
notes: The neighboring modern building at 160 Baldwin is visible immediately beside it.

facade_detail.composition: The street frontage spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys beneath a flat, consistent with its commercial typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The visible opening pattern is organized as 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 2 principal entrances.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Key storefront and facade features include cornice, decorative brick.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Constructed between 1924 and 1930, the property has retained its brick cladding, second storey window openings with cast stone sills and brick surrounds, cornice, and cast stone capped pediment with stone coat of arms. The property has value as an early-twentieth-century vernacular style two-storey commercial addition to a nineteenth-century residential structure.

facade_description: Vividly repainted two-storey commercial building (House of Moses) with a bright yellow-painted brick facade and green-red trim accents. The ground floor features a retail storefront with one white-painted side door and one glazed entry beneath an arched sign panel. Two symmetrically placed domestic-style windows on the upper storey retain cast stone sills and brick surrounds from the 1924-1930 construction. A modest projecting cornice caps the flat-roofed parapet. The neighbouring modern building at 160 Baldwin is visible immediately beside it.
heritage_summary: Constructed between 1924 and 1930, the property has retained its brick cladding, second storey window openings with cast stone sills and brick surrounds, cornice, and cast stone capped pediment with stone coat of arms. The property has value as an early-twentieth-century vernacular style two-storey commercial addition to a nineteenth-century residential structure.
